ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general bibimbap entry (FOODON_00002572) but no tuna-specific bibimbap variant; closest_parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FKP covers bibimbap as a Korean mixed rice dish; no tuna-specific facet available; closest_parent applied.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept exists for bibimbap or tuna bibimbap as a food entity.

main_ingredients: assorted vegetables (spinach, bean sprouts, carrots, zucchini, mushrooms)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of tuna bibimbap is approximately 450 g, comprising a bowl of steamed rice topped with tuna, assorted seasoned vegetables, a fried or raw egg yolk, gochujang sauce, and sesame oil.

nutrient_source.title: Bibimbap with meat and vegetables — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy / FAO INFOODS SEA component estimation
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for tuna bibimbap specifically. Values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central data for bibimbap (NDB 35188 / SR Legacy) and USDA data for canned tuna in water (NDB 15121), weighted to reflect a typical tuna bibimbap composition: approximately 55% steamed rice, 20% canned tuna, 20% mixed seasoned vegetables, 5% sauce/oil. Core macros cross-checked against FAO/INFOODS SEA mixed rice dish ranges. Confidence is medium.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — white rice mixed dish analogous estimate; Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for tuna bibimbap. GI estimated at 54 based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for mixed Korean rice dishes and the established GI-lowering effect of protein (tuna), fat (sesame oil), and fibre (vegetables) on white rice GI. Plain white rice GI is approximately 72; the addition of protein, fat, and vegetable fibre in bibimbap proportions reduces the composite GI to approximately 54, consistent with published mixed rice dish GI values (Atkinson et al., Diabetes Care 2021). GL per serving = (54 × 17.0 × 4.5) / 100 = 41.3 g·units, rounded to one decimal. gi_category is low per the ≤55 threshold rule.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from gochujang and seasoned vegetables
hypertension.guidance: Tuna bibimbap contains approximately 310 mg sodium per 100 g (approximately 1395 mg per standard 450 g serving), which represents a substantial portion of the 1500–2000 mg daily sodium target for hypertensive individuals; patients should request reduced-sauce or low-sodium preparation and limit portion size.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from white rice base, partially offset by protein and fibre
type2_diabetes.guidance: With an estimated GI of 54 and a glycemic load of approximately 41 per serving, tuna bibimbap has a moderate glycaemic impact; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should monitor portion size, consider substituting brown rice, and pair the dish with additional non-starchy vegetables to further blunt postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Omega-3 fatty acids from tuna and low saturated fat content
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Tuna provides EPA and DHA omega-3 fatty acids which support cardiovascular health; the dish has a low saturated fat content (approximately 0.5 g per 100 g), making it a heart-friendly choice when sodium intake is managed through reduced gochujang.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Tuna bibimbap (참치비빔밥, 金枪鱼拌饭) is a Korean mixed rice bowl dish in which steamed white rice is topped with canned or fresh tuna, an assortment of seasoned and blanched vegetables such as spinach, bean sprouts, julienned carrots, zucchini, and shiitake mushrooms, a fried or raw egg yolk, and a spoonful of gochujang (Korean fermented chilli paste), finished with sesame oil and sesame seeds. The dish is assembled in a bowl and mixed thoroughly before eating, creating a harmonious blend of savoury, spicy, nutty, and umami flavours with varied textures ranging from tender rice and flaky tuna to crisp vegetables. Tuna bibimbap is a popular and accessible variant of the classic bibimbap, widely served in Korean restaurants and fast-casual Korean eateries across Singapore and Southeast Asia. Nutritionally, it provides a balanced macronutrient profile with moderate protein from tuna (approximately 7.2 g per 100 g), complex carbohydrates from rice, and beneficial omega-3 fatty acids. The dish has a moderate glycaemic load due to the white rice base, partially mitigated by the protein, fat, and dietary fibre from tuna, vegetables, and sesame oil. Sodium content is a clinical consideration due to gochujang and seasoning. It is suitable for halal diets and is dairy-free. Clinically, it is a reasonable meal choice for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes when portion size is controlled and brown rice substitution is considered.
common_user_queries: How many calories in tuna bibimbap?
Is tuna bibimbap good for diabetics?
Is tuna bibimbap high in sodium?

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for tuna bibimbap as a named dish. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central bibimbap and canned tuna entries using a weighted component estimation approach. GI is an analogous estimate based on published mixed Korean rice dish data from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2021). Confidence is medium rather than high because exact dish-level data from HPB Singapore Foodo or ICMR-NIN is not available for this specific preparation.
notes: Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the adapted USDA source data for this dish composition. Trans fat is reported as 0.0 g per 100 g; this is confirmed zero based on the ingredient profile (no hydrogenated fats used). Nutrient values represent a typical restaurant-style tuna bibimbap serving and may vary depending on the proportion of tuna, rice, and vegetables used by individual restaurants. The field survey data source (Mincheng Bibimbap 민정비빔밥 敏丁拌 - Funan) was used only for food identity and price data; it was not used as a nutritional source. GI set to 54 as an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database mixed rice dish data; gi_category is low per the ≤55 threshold rule.

Iteration 2: Verified all hard gates and Q1–Q8 criteria. Confirmed gi_category = 'low' per ≤55 threshold (GI 54 ≤ 55). Confirmed all aliases_by_language strings present verbatim in flat aliases. Confirmed trans_fat_g = 0.0 is justified (no hydrogenated fats). Confirmed vitamin_d_mcg = -1 documented in notes. All gates pass, score 8/8.
